schools.summary: Robertson defends the reading 'the Lord'; Ellicott and Clarke hear the clause as the informers' very words; the Pharisees who broke John now turn on Jesus.
Gill hears omniscience in 'the Lord knew'; Calvin reads the withdrawal as a rule of vocation — neither fearful retreat nor rash exposure.
Henry suspects the report exaggerated, and finds a good sign in enemies enraged at the gospel's success.
Augustine: Christ fled envy, not danger, to teach that flight from persecution is no sin.
Chrysostom: the retreat pacified envy and guarded the reality of the incarnation from doubt.
Wesley: the Lord knew, 'though none informed him of it.'

debates.question: How did Jesus know what the Pharisees had heard?

sides.position: divine omniscience — none informed him
left open — omniscience or human report

schools.summary: Augustine: he baptized in that he cleansed, baptized not in that he dipped not; Alcuin: the Spirit could be possessed secretly before Pentecost.
Chrysostom: Jesus withheld water-baptism because the Spirit was not yet given; the disciples' baptism only brought men to Christ.
Ellicott: the parenthesis corrects the report, not the writer; Robertson: the imperfect makes non-baptizing Jesus' habit, unmentioned again till the Great Commission.
Henry gives six reasons Christ baptized not, chiefly to show sacraments do not depend on the administering hand.
Calvin: baptism's power hangs on its Author, not its minister — the argument against Anabaptists as against Donatists.
Robertson weighs the verb 'left' and John's design of supplementing the Synoptics; Ellicott defends 'again' and dates the start of the Galilean ministry here.
Gill: after some eight months in Judea Jesus withdrew, not in fear but to avoid provoking the Pharisees before his time.
Wesley: he left to shun the effects of their resentment.
Henry: an example of 'flee to another city' — we may change our place, though not our religion.
Chrysostom: as the expelled apostles went to the Gentiles, so Christ goes toward the Samaritans — but only in passing.
Alcuin allegorizes: leaving Judea is leaving unbelief; entering Galilee, the fickle world — a passage from vices to virtues.

outliers.note: Reads the itinerary mystically where all others read strategy and chronology.

schools.summary: The necessity is geographic: the direct road from Judea to Galilee ran through Samaria, the Galileans' customary festival route per Josephus.
Gill adds a deeper necessity — the effectual calling of a woman and other Samaritans given to Christ by the Father.
Henry holds road and errand together: no other way, yet a lost sheep to be sought; hasten through places of temptation.
Chrysostom recounts Samaria's history — Assyrian colonists half-converted, worshipping God alongside their idols.

debates.question: Does 'must needs' name mere geography or divine purpose?

sides.position: the only proper road — a plain geographic necessity
a necessity of grace: the calling of the Samaritans lay within his design

outliers.note: Illustrates the route with Biddulph's 1601 travel journal of the road from Galilee.

schools.summary: Divided on Sychar: Ellicott argues for a distinct village (modern Askar); Barnes and Robertson weigh both identifications; all rehearse the patriarchal deeds to the parcel of ground.
Gill and Calvin take Sychar as Shechem under an altered name, Calvin citing Jerome's suspicion of the reading.
Clarke derives the name from 'drunken', Isaiah's charge against Ephraim; Wesley simply equates Sychar with Shechem.
Theophylact: the ground is named to explain the woman's boast and to show Gentiles succeeding Jews is nothing new; Chrysostom recalls Dinah's avenging there.
Alcuin: the parcel was left to Christ, whom Joseph typified — the legal heir now visits his inheritance.

debates.question: Is Sychar Shechem itself or a separate village?

sides.position: Shechem under a corrupted or contemptuous name
a distinct place, surviving in the village Askar near the well

schools.summary: Robertson notes spring versus dug well; Ellicott and the travellers' reports fix the site; the descriptive imperfect bears an eyewitness stamp.
Augustine: strong because the Word, weak because the Word made flesh; with Alcuin he counts the sixth hour as the world's sixth age.
Calvin: the weariness was real, fitting him to be a high priest touched with our infirmities.
Henry: a true man, a poor man, a tender man — toil came in with sin and Christ submitted to it.
Chrysostom: 'thus' means on the bare ground, wearied, waiting for the disciples.

debates.question: What time is 'the sixth hour'?

sides.position: Jewish reckoning: about noon — heat, thirst, and the dinner errand
Roman reckoning: about six in the evening, the usual hour for drawing water

outliers.note: Allegorizes the well's depth as the earth into which Christ descended in the world's sixth age.

schools.summary: Chrysostom clears Jesus of inconsistency — the meeting arose by chance; Theophylact insists the thirst was real.
Gill: her coming was providence, not chance — a chosen vessel while Judea's wise were passed by; Calvin: real thirst, yet her salvation preferred to his own wants.
Augustine: he thirsted for her faith; the foreign woman is a type of the Gentile Church about to be justified.
A woman of the country, not the city; the historical present, Rebekah's parallel, and the customs of women at wells.
Wesley: one conversation brought her to knowledge the apostles were long in attaining.

debates.question: Did the woman come by chance or by providence?

sides.position: quite by chance — Jesus did not plan the encounter
by the providence of God, ordering all by his purpose

schools.summary: Lightfoot sets the anathema against the Talmud's own loophole on Cuthite victuals; Ellicott notes Galilean laxity; Robertson and Ellicott parse tense and 'food.'
Gill: the absence was opportune — Christ spared his disciples offence and the woman public shame.
Chrysostom: no store of food, no attendants — the Master training the newly exalted to humility.
Henry: Christ paid for his meat, filled the catering-time with soul-work, and could dine by a draw-well contentedly.
Wesley: the note simply explains the request — else he needed not have asked her.

debates.question: Why does the Evangelist mention the disciples' absence?

sides.position: simply to explain the request — else he needed not have asked her
to show Christ chose their absence for a private, unshaming dealing with the woman

outliers.note: Quotes the threefold Samaritan anathema of Ezra's day in full from the rabbinic sources.

schools.summary: Gill and Calvin hear a jeering reproach; Calvin indicts both her wounded pride and the Jews' zeal worn as a cloak for carnal hatred.
The explanatory clause is the Evangelist's note; Lightfoot argues 'no dealings' overstates a narrower refusal of communion; Ellicott and Robertson supply the Samaritans' history and the curses.
Wesley hears open simplicity, not malice; Clarke weighs dress and dialect and the Jews' 'most merciful wish' of annihilation.
Chrysostom: she knew him by figure and speech; Christ asked what custom disallowed on purpose, to abolish such observances.
Augustine: Jews would not use Samaritan vessels — hence her astonishment at his asking to drink from hers.

debates.question: Who says 'Jews have no dealings with Samaritans'?

sides.position: the Evangelist's explanatory note for Greek readers
more naturally the woman's own jeer

debates.question: Is her astonishment mockery or simplicity?

sides.position: scoffing reproach
open simplicity or plain surprise

schools.summary: Ellicott's role-reversal reading; Robertson's unfulfilled condition and the gift as the gift of John 3:16; Jacob's well itself only a cistern.
Clarke and Wesley: the free gift is Christ, the living water the Spirit and its fruits — allegory drawn from nature's simplest necessities.
Augustine: living water flows from a spring and dies when cut from its fountain-head; Christ thirsted for her faith.
Chrysostom: fire and water name the operations of one grace — consuming sin, cleansing and refreshing souls.
Origen: no one receives a divine gift who does not ask — even the Son is bidden to ask.
Calvin: unmerited calling made visible — Isaiah's 'found by them that sought me not'; knowledge of the gift begins in known poverty.

debates.question: What is 'the gift of God'?

sides.position: Christ himself, God's free gift to the world
the present opportunity of salvation, or the living water itself

schools.summary: The practical objection detailed — no rope, a hundred-foot shaft, the kept bucket; John's word for the well shifts from spring to pit.
Gill and Calvin: 'Sir' is common civility; she still disdains and even mocks, answering figure with jibe.
Chrysostom: the words raised her notions of Christ — she now thinks him no common person.
Augustine: she reads living water as well-water and sets up the comparison with Jacob that follows.

debates.question: Does her 'Sir' mark dawning reverence or continued mockery?

sides.position: a changed tone — she feels the latent force of his words
mere civility masking scorn — she laughs in her sleeve

schools.summary: Gill unmasks the 'mixed medley' behind the boast; Calvin: she compares the servant to the master — as Papists boast of Fathers they do not follow.
The claim ran through Joseph and Ephraim; her question expects the answer no; Ellicott explains why the well was the Samaritans' one unbroken continuity.
Clarke grants the ten-tribes ancestry but calls the current race corrupted; Wesley: 'so they fancied.'
Chrysostom: she thrusts herself upon the Jewish stock; Theophylact: 'and his cattle' commends the water's abundance.
Bede's kinder gloss: she claims Jacob as father because she lives under Moses' law on Jacob's ground.
Origen allegorizes: the well is Scripture — the learned drink as Jacob's sons, the simple as his cattle.

debates.question: Had the Samaritans any right to call Jacob 'our father'?

sides.position: none — a planted, mixed people boasting a borrowed pedigree
a qualified right — dwellers on Jacob's ground under Moses' law, through Joseph's tribes

outliers.note: Alone turns the well itself into an allegory of Scripture and its readers.

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Jesus implies rather than asserts his superiority to Jacob, and depreciates nothing — thirst simply returns; fire and water name grace's operations.
Barnes: an answer by inference, not assertion; Robertson: the present participle — keep on drinking — against the once-for-all draught to come.
Augustine: the well is the pleasure of the world, drawn with the waterpot of lust, and it always leaves one thirsty again.
Gill notes the patient gentleness under scoffing; Calvin: one water serves the decaying body for a time, the other quickens the soul eternally.

outliers.note: Reads the well allegorically as worldly pleasure while the rest stay with the natural fact.

schools.summary: Wesley: an inward fountain becoming an ocean of streams — yet never thirsting only 'provided he continue to drink'; Clarke chains life, Spirit, and indwelling Christ.
The strongest possible negative; water that leaps up; the implied answer that only one greater than Jacob could promise it.
Gill: the water is the Spirit and his grace; Calvin: a continual watering that overflows into blessed immortality.
Cyril: a new kind of water, the Spirit promised to believers; Theophylact: grace's seed grows by the saints' own cultivation.

debates.question: Can the one who drinks ever thirst again?

sides.position: perseverance conditioned on continuing to drink — thirst returning is the man's fault
a perennial fountain that will never fail the renewed

schools.summary: Henry states the dilemma honestly: taunting ridicule, or a well-meant but weak and ignorant desire — hard to say.
Calvin: she understands but despises — doctrine cannot enter while the speaker's authority is denied.
Robertson: half-ironical yet puzzled and attracted; Lightfoot: scoff, not supplication; Ellicott dismisses Godet's 'passing by' conjecture.
Chrysostom traces her ascent by degrees — she now prefers Christ to the patriarch Jacob, though not yet knowing the gift.
Augustine: carnal but sincere — if Elijah could be kept from thirst forty days, why not for ever?
Clarke: her curiosity was excited by design, preparing her mind for the truths to come.

debates.question: Is 'give me this water' jest or earnest?

sides.position: a scoffing challenge
a sincere though carnal and ignorant desire
genuinely ambiguous — half irony, half attraction

schools.summary: No change of subject but the appropriate remedy: Christ strikes the scoffer's conscience with conviction of sin, the ploughshare that must break hard ground before doctrine can profit.
A deliberate, delicate-yet-pungent method: by showing her that he knew her life, Jesus proves himself qualified to teach her and prepares her to own him Messiah.
Clarke's two purposes — make her consider her state, show that he knew her heart; Wesley: he strikes directly at her bosom sin.

outliers.note: Conjectures that she may have started to leave, that her trips to the well were partly for her husband, and that part of the conversation may be unpreserved.

schools.summary: Her four true words are an evasion: the Greek word veils the difference between her 'man' and a legal husband, and Jesus reads the double sense as only he can.
Gill lets the answer be candid truth spoken to a stranger; Calvin has Christ press the ulcer more sharply, reading her past as divorce upon divorce ending in fornication.
Wesley: in all our Lord's discourses, the utmost weightiness and yet the utmost courtesy.

debates.question: Is 'I have no husband' candid confession or veiled evasion?

sides.position: an evasion — true words meant to convey an untruth
a plain truth, spoken freely because a stranger heard it and it served her purpose

outliers.note: Reconstructs her history: a froward, disobedient wife who constrained her husbands to divorce her, then fell to open fornication.

schools.summary: They divide over her guilt: Lightfoot reads five divorces for adultery and a sixth man who is an adulterer; Barnes leaves death, divorce, or desertion open but calls the present union unlawful; Ellicott holds death and sin together and stresses that Christ knows all.
Gill: possibly five lawful marriages ended by death, 'or rather' five divorces for adultery — the one cause of divorce the Samaritans, holding only Moses' law, admitted.

Clarke will not call her a prostitute — the adulteress was put to death, not divorced — and airs Pearce's reading that no reproof is intended; Wesley leaves the question to her own awakened conscience.

debates.question: How guilty do the five husbands and the sixth man prove her?

sides.position: divorced five times for her adulteries; the man she now has is an adulterer
the marriages may have been lawful and the divorces blameless or capricious; Pearce even reads the sixth as a betrothed not yet brought home, with no reproof intended
the histories stay open, but the present connection is unlawful

outliers.note: Collects the tradition's exaggerations — Heracleon gives her six husbands, Jerome twenty-two.
Brings rabbinic evidence that 'the Cuthites do not understand the law about betrothings and divorcings.'

schools.summary: Chrysostom: unoffended by the rebuke, her admiration rises, and she asks nothing of this life — she is eager for doctrine.
Augustine: the 'husband' — the understanding — is beginning to come to her; she thought him a prophet, and he was.
'Prophet' here means not a foreteller but one who read her heart and life; Robertson: 'I am beginning to perceive,' with the emphasis on 'thou.'
Calvin: the fruit of the reproof — repentance is the commencement of docility and opens the gate of Christ's school; Gill notes her changed countenance and tone.
Clarke: she thought him qualified to settle the great dispute, though not yet Messiah; Wesley: so soon was her heart touched.

debates.question: Does her confession lead her to evade or to inquire?

sides.position: glad to turn the talk from her life to a speculative religious dispute
genuinely awakened — eager for doctrine and desiring instruction of her own accord

outliers.note: Continues his allegory of the summoned husband: the understanding, not yet fully come, beginning to be present in her.

schools.summary: They reconstruct the claim: patriarchal altars at Shechem, Sanballat's rival temple destroyed by Hyrcanus, and a Samaritan Pentateuch altered at Deuteronomy 27:4.
Augustine stages the great dispute: did our fathers who pleased God worship in that temple, or in this mountain?
Chrysostom: by 'our fathers' she means Abraham, said to have offered up Isaac here.
Origen turns the quarrel to etymology: Gerizim 'division' befits the Samaritans, type of heretics; Sion 'watch-tower' the Jews, type of true believers.
Calvin: she sincerely consults about pure worship, and the Samaritans' plea from the fathers becomes his treatise on false imitation — patriarchal liberty ended when God fixed his place.
Clarke and Wesley supply the history of Sanballat's temple for the expelled Manasseh; Wesley thinks her question the most important of all.

debates.question: Why does she raise the Gerizim-or-Jerusalem question?

sides.position: to deflect — she will speak of anything rather than of self
a sincere inquiry after the pure worship of God — 'a mistaken opinion' to call it cunning

debates.question: Is the Samaritan reading 'Gerizim' at Deuteronomy 27:4 a corruption or the true text?

sides.position: a notorious partisan falsification
Kennicott, reported by Clarke, strongly contends Gerizim is the genuine reading

outliers.note: Adds the Talmud's satire: the mountain holy because not overflown by the deluge — 'a doughty reason indeed!'

schools.summary: Chrysostom: faith is the sacred anchor demanded; a greater doctrine is revealed to her than to Nicodemus or Nathanael, and both places lose their priority in dignity.
Alcuin: 'the hour cometh' is the Gospel dispensation, shadows of types withdrawing before truth; Augustine: 'believe me' fits now that the husband — the understanding — is present.
Worship is emancipated from bondage to place — not abolished at Jerusalem or Gerizim, but no longer confined to either.
Calvin: Moses' order was for a time; the partition-wall comes down, and 'Father' quietly supplants the 'fathers' she had pleaded — a common Father to all.
Wesley: 'believe me' occurs only here, and to a Samaritan; Clarke: 'the Father' shows the mild, tender nature of the Gospel dispensation.
Ellicott defends the clause as certainly original and thoroughly Hebrew; Robertson: the Samaritans knew whom to worship but did not know him, and the Messiah, God's Son, was a Jew.
Chrysostom: they worshipped a local, partial God mingled with idols; Christ counts himself with the Jews in condescension, and 'salvation' includes his own presence in the flesh.
Calvin: without knowledge from the word we worship a phantom, not God — all 'good intentions' struck as by a thunderbolt.
Wesley sets their fear of the Lord beside the service of their own gods; Clarke holds the idolatry was laid aside after Sanballat's temple, the defect lying in the rejected prophets.
Augustine: 'we know what we worship' is spoken not for the reprobate Jews but for the party from whom apostles and prophets came.
Origen mystically: 'ye' are all who read Scripture in an heretical sense; 'we' the Word and those conformed to his image.

debates.question: In what sense did the Samaritans worship 'they know not what'?

sides.position: their worship was still entangled with idolatry — a local, partial god
they worshipped the true God ignorantly, the idolatry long laid aside — their defect was rejecting the prophets

debates.question: What is the 'salvation' that is of the Jews?

sides.position: the Saviour, the Messiah himself
the whole saving knowledge of God flowing from the Jews, of which Christ's presence is part

outliers.note: Reads 'ye' and 'we' mystically — heretical readers of Scripture over against the Word and those conformed to his image.

schools.summary: 'And now is' could not be said in v.21: spiritual independence of place begins at once, and the Father is pictured seeking worshippers — a doctrine running through the Gospel.
Chrysostom: no far prediction — the event approaches your very doors; the 'true' worshippers are marked off from the false; Cyril cites the verse to teach worship of God in the Spirit.
Origen: two hours — a perfect, purely spiritual worship and an earthly one — and a 'Jerusalem' which is the Church, itself destined to pass.
Calvin: worship is the inward faith of the heart; the Law's worship was spiritual in substance, carnal in form — and Popery has rebuilt the shadows thicker than the Jewish.
Clarke: worship must spring from the heart through the influence of the Holy Ghost; Wesley: not here or there only, but at all times and in all places.

debates.question: Does 'in spirit' mean the worshipper's inward heart or worship wrought by the Holy Spirit?

sides.position: the human spirit — mind, soul, heart — opposed to rites and external pomp
worship animated by the Holy Ghost, the human spirit being his shrine

outliers.note: Finds two hours and an allegorical Jerusalem — the Church — where even the angels' worship does not remain; the perfect hour makes worshippers sons.

schools.summary: 'God is spirit' — anarthrous like 'God is light' and 'God is love'; the real 'must' of worship, aimed at a Samaritan theology that already refused anthropomorphism.
Gill unfolds the divine simplicity and will even read 'the Spirit is God'; Calvin holds the sentence to its point — the Father's spiritual nature — and calls its anti-Arian use a strain.
Chrysostom: the incorporeal God is worshipped with the soul, not the body; Theophylact: 'in truth' guards against heretics who worship with the mind alone.
Origen: God is Spirit as constituting our real life, as breath constitutes the body's.
Hilary: the liberty and uncircumscribed nature of spiritual worship; Augustine: descend that you may ascend — pray in yourself, but first become the temple of God.
Clarke: among the most sublime and necessary truths in the compass of nature; Wesley: worship suitable to his nature — faith, love, and holiness animating all.

debates.question: What may 'God is a Spirit' be made to prove?

sides.position: it reaches the whole Trinity — Gill will even read 'the Spirit is God,' the description agreeing with each divine person
it declares simply the Father's spiritual nature grounding spiritual worship — improper to strain it against the Arians

outliers.note: Corroborates the doctrine from the East, quoting Abu'l Fazel and Creeshna's Geeta on worship in holiness of spirit.

schools.summary: The Samaritan Messianic hope rested on the books of Moses they owned; 'which is called Christ' is the evangelist's gloss, and her present tense 'Messias cometh' trembles on the edge of a guess.
Chrysostom grounds the expectation in Shiloh and the Prophet like Moses; Cyril of Jerusalem folds the passage into his catechesis on the Holy Ghost.
Gill reads honest indecision referred to the Messiah's coming; Calvin hears a compressed confession that the Law gave only first principles and that Christ's doctrine is final.
Clarke's manuscripts read 'we know' — all Samaria expected the advent from promises in Moses; Wesley hears joy and desire of fuller instruction.
Augustine plays on Messias/Christ/Unctus — she knew who could teach her but not Who was teaching her — and finds the five senses at last giving way to the name of Christ.
Origen defines the very word 'Gospel' as glad promise, and recalls Samaria's counterfeit christ Dositheus.

debates.question: Whence did the woman and her people learn to look for 'Messias'?

sides.position: from the books of Moses themselves — Shiloh and the Prophet like Moses
the name Messias is absent from the Pentateuch; the Samaritans, like the Sadducees, likely used the Prophets privately while barring them from public reading

outliers.note: Raises the Sadducee comparison and the rabbinic rules of synagogue reading to explain how a Pentateuch-only people knew the name Messias.
Digresses to define the word 'Gospel' itself and records the Samaritan pseudo-christ Dositheus.

themes: he will tell us all things

schools.summary: The first open profession of Messiahship — matched in plainness only by John 9:37 — made safely in Samaria, where no envy or political fire could catch.
Clarke (with Bishop Pearce) and Wesley explain the reserve toward the Jews: a plain claim there would have bred insurrection or accusation before his hour.
Chrysostom: disclosed now and not at first, lest it seem vanity — open to the simple-hearted woman, withheld from those who asked only to do him injury.
Gill marks the opportunity taken; Calvin reads the confession as Christ assuming his office of Teacher, the name Messiah a seal ratifying the gospel (Isaiah 61:1).
The Greek says 'with a woman': the shock was a rabbi conversing publicly with any woman, against rabbinic precept; the imperfect tense keeps them staring — and silent.
Wesley and Clarke widen the marvel — a woman, and of the hateful nation; Clarke adds the abiding eastern custom against speaking even to one's own wife in the street.
Calvin traces the wonder to two causes, both reverent and both wrong, and makes their unasked question a rule of modest silence before what God has not yet revealed.
Augustine acquits the disciples: they marvelled at his goodness — he who came to seek the lost was seeking the lost one — and suspected no evil.
Chrysostom: for all their wonder they asked nothing, so great was their awe and their care to keep the rank of disciples.
Henry suspects something amiss in the marvelling, like the Pharisees at the publicans, yet praises their silent acquiescence in whatever Christ says and does.

debates.question: Was the disciples' astonishment blameless?

sides.position: they marvelled at his goodness and suspected no evil
perhaps something amiss in it — akin to the Pharisees' offence at his eating with publicans and sinners

outliers.note: Documents the rabbinic maxims through the Berurea stories — 'do not multiply discourse with a woman,' 'a woman ought not to be wise above her distaff.'

schools.summary: Calvin: the forgotten pitcher expresses the ardor of her zeal — faith by nature wishes others to share — and she was no usurping teacher, only a trumpet or bell calling men to Christ.
Excitement and haste in the ingressive aorist (the pot too large for speed anyhow); Ellicott reads the abandoned vessel as a pledge of return and a mark of the eyewitness.
Chrysostom: kindled to fire, she leaves the errand itself; as the apostles left their nets, she leaves her waterpot and evangelizes a whole city unbidden.
Augustine allegorizes the waterpot as the love of this world left behind — would-be preachers must first leave their waterpots at the well.
Origen: she lays aside low bodily cares and her former desires — giving up the sixth man for a seventh, she is converted to chastity.
Henry holds all the readings at once: kindness to Christ, haste with good tidings, and a holy carelessness of the world.
Clarke and Wesley: so penetrated with the truths she heard that she forgot her errand — 'forgetting smaller things.'

debates.question: Why did she leave the waterpot?

sides.position: haste, excitement, and the forgetfulness of zeal
kindness — that Jesus and his disciples might have wherewithal to drink
a figure of renounced worldliness and concupiscence

outliers.note: Reads the left waterpot as a pledge of her return and a quiet mark of the eyewitness who related the incident.

schools.summary: Chrysostom: 'come, see' asks the easier thing and trusts the well itself to convince; Cyril points the antithesis — the chief priests knew him not, a woman of Samaria proclaimed him.
Alcuin: she preaches by degrees, first calling him only 'a man,' lest anger forestall the hearing.
The particle expects no 'yes' — a hesitant, tactful question that piques curiosity; Robertson thinks 'all that ever I did' the exaggeration of a guilty conscience.

Calvin defends her from lying — Christ had compassed her whole life in few words — and admires zeal that spares not her own reputation; Gill: 'come,' for she means to return with them, desiring more of Christ.
Wesley: Christ said little, but her awakened conscience told her the rest; Clarke cites the Jewish test that Messiah must tell the secrets of hearts — fatal to Barchochab.

debates.question: Is 'he told me all things that ever I did' strictly true?

sides.position: a guilty conscience exaggerates — there is no 'ever' in the Greek
no lie: Christ compendiously showed her whole life, and conscience supplied the rest

outliers.note: Brings the rabbinic doctrine of the Messiah's 'scent' (Isaiah 11:3) and Ben Coziba's fatal failure of the test — the woman argued from Christ's knowledge of her hidden deeds.
Turns the verse into catechetical antithesis: the chief priests knew him not; the devils, and a woman of Samaria, confessed him.

schools.summary: Aorist then imperfect: a rush out of the gate, then a long procession still on the way — during which the discourse of John 4:31-38 was spoken.

Henry: God honored an unlikely witness, as once he sent lepers with tidings to Samaria; they went out to Christ rather than sending for him — and these crowds crossing the fields are the whitening harvest.
Gill: 'the men,' as the Syriac expressly reads — the inhabitants of Sychar left their business to come out.
Clarke: such effect had one woman's simple testimony; they were still coming while the discourse with the disciples passed.
Origen fixes the 'mean while' between the woman's going and the town's coming, and reads the entreaty mystically — feeding the Word lest he fail within them.
Augustine: the disciples, returned from buying food, offer Christ some.
Chrysostom: they all press him at once — not impatience, but love and tenderness toward a wearied Master.
Ellicott would restore 'Rabbi' for the ambiguous 'Master'; Robertson's imperfect — 'they kept beseeching him' — shows concern for his comfort outrunning their surprise.
Gill: all this passed while the woman was telling the city of the wonderful person at Jacob's well.
Wesley: 'in the meantime' — before the people came.

outliers.note: Alone allegorizes the meal: souls need food as bodies do — milk for infants, solid meat for the grown — and the disciples feed the Word that he may abide with his nourishers.

schools.summary: Calvin: no asceticism, but the kingdom of God preferred to the body's comforts — do not lose the causes of life for the sake of life itself; Gill adds a textual crumb from the versions.
Chrysostom and Theophylact: his food is the salvation of men — the Samaritans even now coming to believe — spoken figuratively so that the meaning dawns with greater force.
Origen: a rule for teachers set over the weak, who may always say 'I have meat to eat that ye know not of.'
Emphatic I/ye; 'food' is the very word that bears the weight of John 6:27 and 6:55.
Clarke: earthly substances, rightly understood, are the types and shadows of heavenly things.

outliers.note: Extends the saying into his doctrine of graded spiritual capacities — milk for babes, solid meat for the strong.

schools.summary: Augustine: the woman misunderstood the water; behold, the disciples misunderstand the meat.
A question expecting the answer no — surely no one, surely not the Samaritan woman, brought him food; they take his words in the ordinary sense.
Chrysostom hears reverence in the whisper; Theophylact infers that the Lord customarily received support, dignifying poverty and the maintenance of teachers.
Gill: spoken privately among themselves, yet in his hearing — and he knew what they said.
Clarke: perhaps they wondered whether he had been fed preternaturally, remembering Elijah's ravens and angel and their Lord's wilderness repast.

outliers.note: Supposes the disciples thought of preternatural feeding — Elijah fed by ravens and by an angel, and the angels' ministry of Matthew 4:11.
Draws from the question a doctrine of ministerial support: teachers should depend on others for food, that they may be free for the ministry of the word.

schools.summary: 'My food' is doing the Father's will: Ellicott reads the purpose-verbs and MSS tenses, Robertson traces 'finish' forward to 'It is finished', Barnes sees absorption in God's will conquering fatigue and hunger.

Calvin: Christ's greatest delight was the calling given him — to advance God's kingdom and restore lost souls — and he feels the same zeal for our salvation now; Gill fixes the hearers as the disciples.
Wesley: the meat is what satisfies the soul's strongest appetite; Clarke turns the verse into a charge of ministerial zeal for perishing souls.
Divided over 'four months' — proverb (Barnes) or winter time-note (Ellicott, Lightfoot) — but agreed the whitening fields are the approaching Samaritans.
Chrysostom and Theophylact: the expectant Samaritan crowd is the white field; Christ teaches from the objects at hand for vividness and memory.
Augustine: intent on beginning the work, Christ hastens to send labourers — one harvest at the apostles' feet in Acts, another at the end of the world reaped by angels.
Origen allegorizes consistently: the four months are the four elements of natural life, the harvest the end of the world; fields whiten when the Word lights up Scripture.
Calvin: ripe corn cannot bear delay — men count the days to the earthly harvest but doze over the heavenly wheat; Gill counts eight months spent, four remaining to passover.
Wesley: the Samaritans, ripe for the Gospel, covered the ground; Clarke computes the calendar minutely yet concludes the words are an illustration.

debates.question: Is 'yet four months, and then cometh harvest' a proverb or a note of actual time?

sides.position: a proverbial saying about the interval from sowing to harvest
a literal chronological note fixing the scene in winter (Tebeth/December-January)
impossible to decide, though the Greek leans against the proverb

debates.question: Do the four months admit a literal sense at all?

sides.position: an image drawn from the actual season and the approaching crowd
allegory throughout — four elements of natural life, harvest the end of the world

outliers.note: Shows from the rabbinic calendar that the barley for that harvest was not yet even sown when Jesus pointed to fields 'white already'.
Conjectures that the Samaritans' white raiment helped suggest the image of fields white to harvest.

schools.summary: 'Already' opens the verse: the spiritual reaper need not wait four months; the wages are the joy of gathering souls into eternal life, sower and reapers rejoicing at once.
Wesley: Christ the sower, soul-winners the reapers, rejoicing together in heaven; Clarke: sower and reaper are one person — Christ — with seed-time and harvest on the same day.
Gill allegorizes the sickle-work of the ministry; Calvin insists the promised wages are an undeserved reward confirming justification by faith, and reads sowing and reaping as Law and Gospel.
Cyril of Jerusalem cites the verse in catechesis: eternal life is gained by the preaching of the Gospel.

debates.question: Who is the reaper in this verse?

sides.position: the disciples and ministers reap what Christ sowed
Christ himself, sower and reaper of the same field on the same day

outliers.note: Uses the verse in a catechetical list of texts on how eternal life is gained — by faith and by the preaching of the Gospel.

schools.summary: A proverb known to Greeks, Romans, and Scripture; Ellicott: 'true' in John's deeper sense — the world's bitter saying idealised in the gospel relation of sower and reaper.
Wesley: the prophets and Christ sow, the apostles and succeeding ministers reap; Clarke parades the pagan parallels and preserves Bishop Pearce's reading.

Calvin: the prophets rejoiced in their lifetime but are not now conscious witnesses of the Church; ministers must throw their labours into a common stock without envy; Gill: the common proverb is here verified.

debates.question: Whence comes the proverb 'one soweth and another reapeth'?

sides.position: common Greek and Latin proverbial stock, with Jewish parallels
primarily the Old Testament scriptures

outliers.note: Quotes Virgil's 'Sic vos non vobis' at length and preserves Bishop Pearce's turn — the disciples were 'sent away' to the city chiefly so that a harvest might await their return.

schools.summary: The emphatic 'I sent you' contrasts the reapers with prior labourers — variously the prophets, the Baptist, and Christ; Robertson sees the word proved when Philip later reaped Samaria.
Gill: 'I sent you to reap' is the disciples' commission to preach the Gospel and gather in souls.

debates.question: Who are the 'other men' who laboured?

sides.position: the whole preparatory line — the prophets, the Jewish teachers, John the Baptist, and Christ's own ministry
in the immediate application Christ himself alone (perhaps with the woman), the plural notwithstanding

schools.summary: Clarke: the woman was the first apostle of Christ in Samaria; the repeated phrase hints at a fuller conversation than John records.
Chrysostom: the townsmen reasoned soundly from the woman's self-accusing admiration and believed on her testimony alone, without a sign.
The Jews refused a great prophet's witness while Samaria accepted a woman's; her discreet, frank testimony had its share in the harvest, and the success rebukes our despair.
Calvin: 'believed' is used inaccurately — this is a commencement of faith, docility God honours; Gill fixes the city as Sychar in Samaria.
Origen: the woman's report grounded their belief before the enlightening power of the Word was visible; allegorically, they leave the city of heterodox doctrine to come to Jesus.
Augustine: they knew Christ first by report, then by presence — the woman figures the Church announcing Christ to those still outside the fold.

debates.question: How complete was the faith produced by the woman's word?

sides.position: real, admirable faith — believing without signs and surpassing the sign-fed Jews
a commencement — acknowledgment of a Prophet, perfected only on hearing Christ himself

outliers.note: Allegorizes the forbidden 'city of the Samaritans' as heterodox doctrine, and says the townsmen were 'not yet ready for the third day'.

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the sign-fed Jews expelled Christ while the sign-less Samaritans besought him to stay — envy and vainglory mar everything.
Clarke: no miracle at Sychar because none was needed — where the doctrine is credited, no miracle is necessary.
Ellicott: a Jew accepts the hospitality of Samaria as the next step of their faith; Robertson: two days of continuous revival across the Jew-Samaritan divide.
Gill: at the woman's urging they heard Christ for themselves and were thoroughly persuaded he was the Messiah; the Ethiopic version reads 'all the Samaritans'.

outliers.note: Digresses on why miracles are not wrought now: they served only to establish the doctrines of Christianity where first preached.

schools.summary: Ellicott meditates on the veiled two days — the Fatherhood of God burning in mixed, despised Samaria; Robertson: 'more by much' than v.39, Jesus reaping faster than the woman.
Gill: the versions read 'believed on him' — a larger multitude won by Christ's own preaching; Calvin: the fruit of the stay teaches us never to refrain from advancing God's kingdom.

Robertson studies lalia and the perfect 'we have heard', and defends the first-century title 'Saviour of the world' from the inscriptions; Ellicott strips the text to 'truly the Saviour of the world' and marks the growth from hearing to knowing.

Calvin: their faith now rests on the word of God itself — Christ speaking by his ministers — and the salvation he brought is common to the whole world; Gill: the woman, having tasted grace, could not leave him.
Clarke: the Saviour not of the Jews only, but of the Samaritans and the whole Gentile world.

outliers.note: Mounts an epigraphic defence of 'Saviour of the world' from Deissmann's emperor inscriptions against Bernard's scepticism.

schools.summary: Gill: the two days at Sychar were the means of many conversions before the journey resumed.
'The two days' are those of v.40; the moderns read 'his own country' as Nazareth, which Jesus pointedly avoids for Cana.
Augustine's grim symmetry: two days in Samaria brought faith, the same in Galilee brought none.
Chrysostom: the dishonouring country is Capernaum; Theophylact: the clause explains why Jesus was not always in Galilee.
Henry: prophets are most despised at home — Joseph, David, Jeremiah, and Christ's own kin prove it.
Origen: the prophets' country was Judea, and the proverb reaches even pagan teachers despised by their fellow-citizens.

debates.question: Which is the 'own country' that gave no honour?

sides.position: Nazareth, as in the Synoptic uses of the proverb
Capernaum, where he had most resided
Galilee at large, which believed not
Judea, the country of the prophets

schools.summary: Robertson canvasses all four referents and settles on Galilee as John's meaning; Ellicott rejects every geographical dodge — the honour is brought from without.
Calvin: the country is Nazareth; the proverb states what commonly happens and must not be pressed into universal accuracy.
Clarke and Wesley rest on the Synoptic key — the saying was always spoken of Nazareth.

debates.question: How does the proverb explain the journey into Galilee?

sides.position: he went where he had no fame yet, to labour where honour was absent
he came to his own country only after honour had been won elsewhere — a prophet's friends are the last to hear
he entered Galilee but shunned Nazareth, the one town the proverb fits

outliers.note: Treats the saying as a secular proverb with parallels in common speech, tracing its truth to familiarity and envy, and noting Paul calls even Epimenides a prophet.

schools.summary: The welcome rests on the perfect participle — having seen the unrecorded Jerusalem signs; the feast-going Galileans were predisposed as the Samaritans were not.
Clarke: they received him as the promised Messiah because of the Passover miracles.
Calvin doubts the honour's durability — nothing is more forgotten than God's gifts; miracles serve to prepare the way for doctrine.
Origen: the temple-cleansing had impressed them with Christ's majesty, and the feast providentially prepared their reception.
Henry: diligent attendance on ordinances brought the Galileans more benefit than they expected.
Bede allegorizes the homecoming: at the end of the world the Lord returns to Judea, the Gentiles being confirmed in love.

debates.question: How much was the Galilean welcome worth?

sides.position: a kindly, believing reception of God's messenger, even of the Messiah
a sign-fed honour of uncertain duration

outliers.note: Reads the return to Galilee as a figure of Christ's return to Judea at the end of the world.

schools.summary: The basilikos is a courtier of Herod Antipas — perhaps Chuza or Manaen, proof wanting; Cana chosen for the memory of the first sign.
Chrysostom: Jesus returns to confirm the faith the wine-miracle produced, and proves this man distinct from Matthew's centurion; Theophylact: the reminder honours Samaritan faith by contrast.
Augustine: at the wedding only the disciples believed — he visits again to try a second time.
Calvin: no 'little king' but a gentleman of Herod's court; the rank is named because it makes the miracle more illustrious.

debates.question: Who was the nobleman?

sides.position: a courtier of Herod Antipas — perhaps Chuza or Manaen
possibly an officer of Caesar's household on commission

outliers.note: Makes him an Herodian by conviction, tying the family to the party politics of Matthew 22:16.

schools.summary: The fame had spread twenty-five miles; the tenses show a man who went at once and kept on begging; his faith was real but bound Christ to bodily presence.
Henry: sincerity and infirmity mixed — like Naaman he expects the healer's hand; we may pray but not prescribe.
Gill notes the spreading fame; Calvin: applying to Christ was faith, limiting his manner of helping was ignorance of anything above a prophet.
Wesley: 'come down' is literal — Cana stood much higher than Capernaum.
Gill hears roughness of reproof; Calvin: the harshness strikes a nation that left no room for the word, craving only wonders — the same disease as today's 'first show miracles.'
The plural 'ye' aims past the nobleman at the Galileans; faith that demands a miracle is not faith but sight; Robertson hears disappointment at being taken for a mere wonder-worker.
Wesley and Clarke set the Samaritans' wordless-sign faith against Galilee; Clarke: the man had faith, only not enough, and miracle-grace is not saving grace.
Augustine: the request was near a test of Christ's power; the believer's mind should rise above seeking even miracles, which are wrought on mutable things.

debates.question: How much faith did the nobleman have when rebuked?

sides.position: some, but not enough — else he had not come at all
lukewarm, cold, or possibly none — a testing of Christ

outliers.note: Aims the rebuke at Herod's court in particular, which discounted the Baptist because he did no miracle.

schools.summary: Calvin: a courtier who takes roughness in modest silence — adversity had subdued the pride we all share; the reproof corrected, it did not reject.
Henry: patient under reproof yet wrestling on; but he thinks Christ can heal the sick, not raise the dead, and hurries him as if he might slip his time.
Gregory: the deficiency named — he thought the Lord could not save unless personally present.
Chrysostom: an earthly mind hurrying Christ along, perhaps asking in outright unbelief, as desperate fathers try every means.
Robertson with Bengel: power only before death, the assumption of Martha and Mary; Ellicott: sorrow is the birth-pang of faith — weak, but there; the diminutive 'my little child.'

debates.question: Does the renewed plea show budding faith or persisting unbelief?

sides.position: humbled perseverance — faith weak but real, born of sorrow
an earthly mind, possibly asking in unbelief

themes: come down ere my child die

schools.summary: Calvin: astonishing condescension — more granted than asked, that we learn not to prescribe; a half-heathen profits more by one word than crowds by many sermons.
Instantaneous aorist faith acting itself out in the imperfect 'started on his way'; Ellicott: he now believes on Christ's own word, no longer on testimony.
Clarke: the refusal to go down was the cure of unbelief — Christ heals absent by the energy that fills heaven and earth; yet the faith may still have hung on a condition.
Gregory: present where invited even when absent, saving by command as he created by will — and a blow to pride, who walked for the servant but not for the courtier's son.
Chrysostom: the Lord offered to visit the centurion of confirmed faith, refused the nobleman of imperfect faith — and by refusing enlightened him.

debates.question: How complete was the faith with which he 'believed the word'?

sides.position: instantaneous, genuine trust in the bare word
real but conditional and incomplete until confirmed

schools.summary: Ellicott walks the anxious road home; Robertson traces the ladder of words for the boy — son, little child, and the servants' familiar 'thy boy.'
Origen: servants sent out to meet their master display his rank.
Chrysostom: the servants came to cancel the visit — the household still thought of Christ as a physician no longer wanted.
Gill dates the descent to the next day; Calvin: the word's efficacy met by faith's effect — God's power stands ready where unbelief does not shut the door, and his delays make great haste.
Gill: he never doubted, but compared; Calvin: a secret impulse of God made the miracle conspicuous beyond the reach of chance.
Ellicott defends Jewish reckoning and dissolves the 'yesterday' puzzle by the sunset day-change; Robertson reads Roman time and the physician's idiom 'took a turn for the better.'
Wesley: exact consideration of God's works increases faith; Clarke suspects a lingering test of natural cause versus Christ's power, and reports Macknight's 7 p.m. argument.
Henry: the precision proves the cure sudden — dictum factum — and its synchronism with the word adorns Providence.

debates.question: What time was 'the seventh hour'?

sides.position: Jewish reckoning, about one in the afternoon — 'yesterday' explained by the sunset day-change
Roman reckoning, about seven in the evening

debates.question: Why did the father ask the hour?

sides.position: to confirm and publish an undoubted miracle
still to sift natural recovery from divine power

schools.summary: Calvin resolves the paradox of a believer beginning to believe: a Jew's general faith, then a particular faith for his son, now saving faith in the Son of God.
Bede: faith has beginning, growth, and maturity; Augustine sharpens it — he did not truly believe when he first petitioned.
The first whole household to believe, forerunner of Crispus; Ellicott finds a second miracle — new life in the father's own spirit; Barnes reads the scene's evidential force.
Clarke: the family, warmed by God's kindness to the child, believed the more easily — in very faithfulness God had afflicted them.

debates.question: When did the nobleman's faith properly begin?

sides.position: it grew by stages from petition to word to confirmation
only now — the first petition was not yet belief

outliers.note: Prints the Talmud's tale of Chanina and Gamaliel's son with Schoettgen's verdict: a fable copied, egg-like, from the Gospel.

schools.summary: Chrysostom: no jot of Scripture is idle — even 'second' is written to exalt the Samaritans, who believed with no miracle at all.
Gill: the second wrought in Cana — many Judean signs stand between the wedding and this healing.
Clarke construes 'again' with the coming out of Judea, and reads the whole chapter as a lesson in particular providence.
Ellicott: 'this again, a second sign' — the family now the unit of Christian life; eight differences prove this no double of the centurion's story.

debates.question: Is this the centurion's servant retold?

sides.position: one story in two forms, as suspected since Irenaeus
two distinct miracles — son not servant, Jew not Gentile, Cana not Capernaum, rebuke not marvel
